content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Admissions and intake-equity regulation (keep structure, fix the gate)'. Early selection at age 10–12 into the 6- and 8-year grammar school tracks is justifiable only if Hungary can sustain politically durable institutional design, ensure between-school equity gains translate into individual-level SES equalization, and maintain implementation continuity across electoral cycles—outcomes Poland achieved partially and lost entirely, demonstrating that the benefits of delayed selection (1 SD gain for at-risk students, improved labor-market outcomes, reduced school segregation) outweigh the benefits of early tracking only if political reversibility risk and individual-SES gains (not achieved in Poland) are weighted equally with test-score gains.
opportunities: Reduced SES-skew in admitted cohorts at the 6/8-year gate, addressing the documented near-zero intake share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils.
Preserves the approximately 10% competency-gain advantage associated with 6/8-year tracks for admitted students, since the track structure itself is untouched.
strengths: Poland's 1999 delayed-tracking reform, which extended comprehensive general education from 8 to 9 years (moving selection from age 12 to age 15), produced PISA score gains of 0.25 SD in mathematics, 0.28 SD in reading, and 0.16 SD in science between 2000 and 2006; students in likely-vocational tracks gained approximately one standard deviation (~100 PISA points).
Between-school equity improved markedly after the 1999 reform: Poland reduced performance disparities between schools from one of the largest in the OECD to one of the smallest.
threats: Implementation by ministerial decree rather than cardinal law leaves the mechanism exposed to reversal by a subsequent government within a single electoral cycle; the scenario does not propose a specific entrenchment design (e.g., cardinal-law status, cross-party pact, or sunset-clause safeguard) to mitigate this.
Fidesz's demonstrated framing of any selection-related change as ideologically totalitarian creates reversal risk even for a narrower admissions-only intervention, not only for structural abolition.
weaknesses: The precise causal decomposition between the 1999 reform's structural effect (unified intake) versus its resource/composition effects (investment and teacher change) remains uncertain; even Jakubowski et al.'s two-thirds/one-third split is an estimate and could shift with different methodologies.

Whether individual-level SES gaps could have been closed by longer-duration exposure to the 1999 reform (the Polish cohort was measured at age 15, not adulthood) or whether structural timing reform alone is insufficient without targeted SES-based support programs remains untested.
